content:
Text classification with Bayes' rule dates to the 1960s spam-filtering and information-retrieval literature, but the modern **Multinomial** and **Bernoulli** variants were articulated clearly once bag-of-words representations became standard in NLP.

**Maron (1961)** and early probabilistic indexing work showed that word probabilities could rank relevant documents.
**McCallum and Nigam (1998)** popularized Multinomial NB for text with explicit event models and smoothing analysis—still cited in tutorials and sklearn docs.

**Bernoulli** event models treat each vocabulary word as an independent binary trial; they suit short texts where absence matters.
Before deep learning, these models powered **spam filters**, **newsgroup classifiers**, and **sentiment baselines** because they trained in one pass over counts.

They remain pedagogically important: they connect **count features**, **maximum likelihood estimation with smoothing**, and **log-probability decision rules**—the same building blocks that reappear in softmax classifiers and language-model training, but with transparent word-level parameters.

timeline.label: Probabilistic indexing
timeline.description: Early IR treats term occurrence as evidence for relevance—precursor to class-conditional word probabilities.

timeline.label: Multinomial event model (1990s)
timeline.description: Multinomial NB with smoothing becomes the standard generative model for document categorization benchmarks.

timeline.label: Production spam filters
timeline.description: Commercial and open-source filters combine NB scores with rules; word-count spam models reach wide deployment.

timeline.label: Neural NLP era
timeline.description: Transformers replace bag-of-words for accuracy, but NB remains the canonical baseline for count-based text classification.
